video_title: Want less sprawl and more urban infill? Try a land value tax!
transcript: Alrighty. Alrighty. Alrighty. Hello everyone. This is Vaults for July 15th, 2026. Want less sprawl and more urban infill? Try a land value tax. I'm your host, David Roberts. Have you ever driven through an American city and wondered why so much land near and around the urban core remains either undeveloped or covered in low value uses like parking lots? Many people point reflexively to zoning and Lord knows I have done many pods over the last few years on the evils of restrictive zoning. But there is more to it than that. You also need to look at the tax code. Property taxes are determined by a mix of two values. The value of the land and the value of the structures on the land. One perverse outcome of this arrangement is that if you own a vacant lot and you put a building on it, you raise your property taxes. The higher the value of the building, the more economic activity or community benefit it produces, the higher your taxes. In effect, property taxes as currently constructed penalize building and reward leaving land underdeveloped. It is perverse. That's why even many cities that have reformed zoning still suffer from this problem. The obvious solution which is gaining momentum in cities and states across the country is to shift the tax burden away from the buildings to the land. The total property tax bill can remain the same i.e. the reform can be revenue neutral but the resulting land value tax will penalize land owners who leave their lots empty and reward those who build. It will incentivize building on the highest value land near urban cores. The idea is simple enough, but the details get pretty hairy pretty quickly. So, we are going to dive into some of those details. With me today, I have Greg Miller, co-founder and director of the Center for Land Economics and a driving force behind the resurgent discourse around land value taxes. And Kitty Klitsky, a city council member who is pushing for adoption of a version of a land value tax in Spokane, Washington, just down the street from me. I'm going to talk with Greg about the conceptual rationale behind land value taxes and Kitty about the challenges of getting them implemented on the ground. I am super excited for this one. So, let's get into it. All right, with no further ado, Greg Miller, Kitty Klitsky, welcome to Vults. Thank you so much for coming. >> Thank you for having me. >> All right, Greg, let's start with you. Um, in the name of keeping my introduction tractably short, I did not get into Henry George, but let's why don't you briefly tell us who Henry George was and what his basic insight was and why that basic insight has come back. >> Yeah. Henry George is a political philosopher, a journalist, whatever you want to frame him as. From the the 19th century in in the 1890s, he lands down in San Francisco and he's asking himself this question. It's the industrial ages. We are producing things faster than ever. And this is supposed to bring about all this progress, all this prosperity. And he's asking himself, why do I see so many people living in such terrible conditions with so much poverty? And so he writes a book called Progress in Poverty. And what he realized is with the industrial age, we had discovered a new frontier. That frontier is everybody moving to an urban core. There's all of a sudden lomeration effects around living near a factory. And because everybody wants to live near a factory, the people who own the land are the people who are able to benefit the most because they can charge increased rent. And as those factories become more productive, as we discover assembly lines and new methods and mechanisms to make factories more productive, so too do the profits and so too do the wages to the workers. And all of that means that the rents can go up even further because now more people want to be near that factory that pays higher. And we see this today, right? We see this in San Francisco in our big cities. That's where we see the highest elomeration and the highest value of land and the highest rents that people pay for units that you know you could go out into a rural area and have a house uh eight times the size of. And so Henry George the political philosopher who understood that land was a big deal. He invents what he calls the land value tax. Why don't we tax this value, this locationational premium um in our cities? And this wasn't a novel idea necessarily.
